evals.name: trigger-positive-explicit
evals.prompt: I'm building a Rust CLI and the error messages are just println! lines on stderr. I want them to be parseable by the agent that runs my tool. What should I do?
evals.expected_output: Skill triggers. Output proposes RFC 9457 application/problem+json envelope with type/title/status/detail/instance plus the three mandatory extensions (retry_after, suggested_fix, code_actions[]), recommends miette + thiserror with JSONReportHandler, explains applicability markers on suggested_fix, and explains TTY/--format selection.
expectations: Mentions RFC 9457 or application/problem+json as the envelope standard
Names all three mandatory extensions: retry_after, suggested_fix, and code_actions
Explains applicability markers (machine_applicable, maybe_incorrect, has_placeholders, or unspecified) on suggested_fix or code_actions
Recommends dual rendering selected by --format flag or TTY detection, not one or the other in isolation
Recommends miette with its dual handlers (JSONReportHandler and GraphicalReportHandler) for the Rust idiom

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md contains application/problem+json media type
answer.md mentions retry_after extension
answer.md mentions suggested_fix extension
answer.md mentions code_actions extension
answer.md names at least one applicability marker token
answer.md references TTY detection or --format flag for renderer selection
answer.md recommends miette for the Rust idiom

evals.name: trigger-positive-implicit
evals.prompt: My Python click tool dumps a 200-line traceback to stderr when the API rate-limits us. Claude keeps giving up instead of retrying. How do I fix this?
evals.expected_output: Skill triggers without the user naming RFC 9457. Output diagnoses the missing retry_after directive and the token cost of the traceback, proposes the structured envelope with retry_after, suggested_fix with an applicability marker, and gives a Rich-based dual renderer.
expectations: Identifies missing retry_after as the specific reason the agent abandons instead of retrying
Explains the token cost or size problem with verbose tracebacks, citing the post's ~75% reduction or tool_result framing
Provides a Python code example using Rich for the human-rendered path
Preserves human rendering alongside JSON — dual output, not replacement

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md identifies retry_after as the missing directive
answer.md quantifies or names the token cost of verbose tracebacks
answer.md references the Rich library for Python rendering
answer.md explains the agent-abandonment failure mode

evals.name: trigger-negative-near-miss-server-errors
evals.prompt: We're returning 500 errors from our REST API and our React frontend doesn't know what to display to users. Should we return a JSON object with the error message and a localized title?
evals.expected_output: Skill does NOT trigger — this is a web API + frontend UX question, not a CLI error output question. The dual consumers are 'human at terminal' and 'LLM agent', not 'frontend code' and 'end user'. RFC 9457 may be mentioned as the correct REST standard, but CLI-specific terminology (retry_after, code_actions, exit_code, isatty) must not appear as if the skill is in scope.
expectations: Skill does not present itself as active (no CLI dual-consumer framing applied to the user's REST/React problem)
Answer addresses REST API and frontend UX concerns, not CLI stderr / exit_code / TTY rendering
If RFC 9457 appears it is presented as the right answer for REST APIs, not as a CLI prescription — incidental mention is acceptable

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md does not use retry_after (CLI-specific extension) in the context of REST/React advice
answer.md does not apply code_actions[] (CLI agent extension) to the REST/React problem
answer.md addresses the REST / frontend domain

evals.name: trigger-negative-logging
evals.prompt: How should I structure my application logs so they're easy to grep through later? Currently using log/slog in Go.
evals.expected_output: Skill does NOT trigger. Application logging is not CLI error output for a dual human+agent consumer. The post explicitly puts telemetry/logging out of scope.
expectations: Answer addresses application logging (structured fields, JSON handler, grep/jq patterns) rather than CLI error envelope design
Skill does not present itself or apply CLI dual-consumer framing to a logging question

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md does not apply the RFC 9457 CLI envelope to an application logging question
answer.md does not mention retry_after in the context of application logging
answer.md does not apply code_actions[] to application logging
answer.md addresses Go slog / structured logging concerns

evals.name: violation-detection-missing-retry
evals.prompt:
Here is the error my Go CLI emits when it hits a rate limit:

error: rate limit exceeded, please try again later
exit status 1

Review this for me.
evals.expected_output: Identifies (1) no retry_after directive, (2) no structured envelope, (3) single-format pretty-only output, (4) no suggested_fix, (5) no docs_url. Cites the post's specific claim that without retry_after agents abandon recoverable work. Proposes the canonical rate-limit envelope with status 429, machine_applicable on suggested_fix, and exit_code 2.
expectations: Flags missing retry_after as the primary violation for a rate-limit error
Flags single-format (pretty-only) output and the absence of a JSON / structured path
Cites the agent-abandonment failure mode from the post explicitly
Produces a corrected envelope that includes status 429 and exit_code 2
Applies an applicability marker (machine_applicable) on the suggested_fix in the corrected envelope

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md flags missing retry_after
answer.md cites the agent-abandonment failure mode
answer.md corrected envelope contains status 429
answer.md includes an applicability marker on the corrected suggested_fix
answer.md corrected envelope includes suggested_fix

evals.name: violation-detection-ambiguous-fix
evals.prompt:
I'm emitting RFC 9457 from my CLI now. Here's the JSON for a missing-library error:
{
  "type": "https://docs.example.com/cli/errors/missing-lib",
  "title": "Missing library",
  "status": 471,
  "detail": "could not find -lssl",
  "instance": "urn:build:abc",
  "suggested_fix": "Install libssl-dev or openssl-devel"
}
Is anything missing?
evals.expected_output: Flags (1) no applicability marker on suggested_fix (without it agents will apply plausible-looking but wrong fixes), (2) no code_actions[], (3) no docs_url, (4) invalid HTTP status 471 (not a registered code), (5) no retry_after (acceptable for non-transient errors but worth noting). Recommends has_placeholders or maybe_incorrect for the platform-conditional fix.
expectations: Flags the missing applicability marker on suggested_fix and explains the wrong-fix failure mode
Recommends has_placeholders or maybe_incorrect for the platform-dependent install instruction
Flags missing code_actions[] as a required extension beyond the free-text suggested_fix
Flags status 471 as invalid (not a registered HTTP status code)

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md names an applicability marker
answer.md flags missing code_actions[] extension
answer.md recommends has_placeholders or maybe_incorrect for the platform-conditional fix
answer.md flags status 471 as an invalid HTTP status code
answer.md cites the wrong-fix agent failure mode

evals.name: generation-go-cobra
evals.prompt: Add a compliant error to my Cobra CLI for the case where the user supplies a config file that doesn't exist. Show me the Go code and the resulting JSON envelope.
evals.expected_output: Produces (1) a Go Problem struct with the five RFC 9457 standard fields, retry_after omitted (not transient), suggested_fix populated with an applicability marker; (2) code_actions[] with at least one entry; (3) format selection by --format flag or isatty; (4) the resulting JSON with application/problem+json shape; (5) a versioned type URI; (6) exit_code set to a non-zero value.
expectations: Go struct or JSON envelope contains all five RFC 9457 standard fields (type, title, status, detail, instance)
code_actions[] array is present in the JSON envelope with at least one entry
An applicability marker appears on suggested_fix or on a code_action entry
Type URI is versioned or points to a docs endpoint with a changelog path
Format selection uses --format flag or isatty / IsTerminal — not hardcoded

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md JSON envelope contains type field
answer.md JSON envelope contains title field
answer.md JSON envelope contains status field
answer.md JSON envelope contains detail field
answer.md JSON envelope contains instance field
answer.md includes code_actions[] in the generated output
answer.md includes suggested_fix in the generated output
answer.md includes an applicability marker on suggested_fix or a code_action
answer.md uses isatty/IsTerminal or --format for renderer selection

evals.name: generation-python-rich
evals.prompt: Write a small Python helper using Rich that emits both the pretty-rendered error and the application/problem+json envelope for a rate-limit error. The retry window is 30 seconds.
evals.expected_output: Produces Python that uses Rich for the pretty path and json.dumps (or equivalent) for the JSON path, selects between them on --format or sys.stderr.isatty(), includes retry_after=30, status=429, exit_code non-zero, suggested_fix with an applicability marker, and code_actions[] with at least one entry.
expectations: Uses Rich library for the human-rendered path
Format selection uses sys.stderr.isatty() or a --format argument — not hardcoded
retry_after equals 30 (the delta-seconds from the prompt)
status is 429
code_actions[] is present in the generated envelope
An applicability marker appears on suggested_fix or a code_action entry

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md imports or uses the Rich library
answer.md uses TTY detection or --format for renderer selection
answer.md sets retry_after to 30 seconds
answer.md sets status to 429
answer.md includes code_actions[] in the generated Python envelope
answer.md includes an applicability marker
answer.md includes suggested_fix in the generated envelope

evals.name: structural-fidelity-out-of-scope
evals.prompt: What's the complete mapping from HTTP status codes to POSIX exit codes I should use in my CLI's error envelope?
evals.expected_output: Skill triggers but answers honestly: the blog post does NOT prescribe a complete table — it only gives the rate-limit example (status 429 → exit_code 2). The skill marks this as out of scope rather than inventing a mapping.
expectations: Explicitly states the post does not prescribe a complete HTTP-to-exit-code table and marks this as out of scope
Does not invent a fabricated mapping table covering multiple status codes
Quotes or cites the one concrete example from the post: status 429 maps to exit_code 2

deterministic_checks.pattern: out of scope|does not prescribe|post is silent|post does not|not defined
deterministic_checks.description: answer.md explicitly marks a complete exit-code table as out of scope
answer.md cites the single canonical example: status 429 maps to exit_code 2

evals.name: structural-fidelity-versioning
evals.prompt: I'm about to ship my CLI with RFC 9457 error envelopes. Anything I should commit to before release so I don't paint myself into a corner?
evals.expected_output: Names the stable-type-URI / versioning requirement from the post: either embed the version in the URI or track a changelog at the documentation endpoint. Also names the three mandatory extensions and the dual-renderer requirement so they don't become breaking changes later.
expectations: Calls out the stable type URI policy — either version the URI or maintain a changelog at the docs endpoint
Names dual rendering (--format or TTY detection) as a release commitment
Names all three mandatory extensions: retry_after, suggested_fix, code_actions

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md addresses stable type URI or versioning policy
answer.md names dual rendering as a release commitment
answer.md names retry_after as a mandatory extension
answer.md names suggested_fix as a mandatory extension
answer.md names code_actions as a mandatory extension

evals.name: generation-code-actions-applicability
evals.prompt: My Rust CLI fails when the user's config has an unknown key. Generate a complete RFC 9457 envelope for this error — I want agents to be able to auto-fix the config. Include code_actions[] with at least two alternatives: one that removes the unknown key, and one that renames it to the closest valid key.
evals.expected_output: Produces a JSON envelope with: all five standard RFC 9457 fields; code_actions[] with at least two entries (delete-key action and rename-key action); applicability markers on each code_action (machine_applicable for delete, maybe_incorrect or has_placeholders for rename); suggested_fix with an applicability marker; a versioned type URI; docs_url field pointing to documentation.
expectations: code_actions[] array contains at least two entries — one for deleting the key, one for renaming/replacing it
Each code_action entry carries an applicability marker distinguishing the safe action from the uncertain one
suggested_fix is present and carries an applicability marker
docs_url field is present pointing to documentation for the error type
The type URI is versioned (contains /v1/ or similar segment) or is at a stable docs endpoint

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md includes code_actions array
answer.md includes at least one applicability marker on a code_action or suggested_fix
answer.md includes suggested_fix
answer.md includes docs_url field
answer.md JSON envelope contains type field
answer.md JSON envelope contains title field
answer.md JSON envelope contains status field
answer.md JSON envelope contains detail field
answer.md JSON envelope contains instance field

evals.name: fidelity-docs-url-and-stable-uri
evals.prompt: The post mentions a docs_url field and a stable type URI policy. What exactly are the rules, and how do they interact? Show me a correct and an incorrect example of a type URI.
evals.expected_output: Accurately describes the stable-type-URI policy from the post: type URIs must not change after release — either embed the version (e.g. /v1/ in the path) or maintain a changelog at the documentation endpoint. Explains docs_url as the pointer to human-readable documentation for the specific error type. Provides at least one correct example (versioned URI) and one incorrect example (volatile URI).
expectations: Correctly distinguishes docs_url (human-readable error documentation) from the type URI (machine-stable identifier)
States the stable URI rule: either version the URI path or maintain a docs changelog — changing a type URI after release is a breaking change for agents
Provides a concrete correct example of a versioned type URI (e.g. containing /v1/ or an immutable path)
Provides a concrete incorrect example that would violate the stable URI policy (e.g. a path that changes on rename/re-org)
Honestly marks any aspect of the policy the post does not specify as out of scope or implementation-defined

deterministic_checks.description: answer.md explains docs_url field
answer.md explains the stable URI policy
answer.md shows or describes a versioned URI
